summary:
The Kamakura Shogunate (1185–1333 AD) was Japan's first military government (bakufu, 'tent government'), established by Minamoto no Yoritomo at the city of Kamakura in eastern Japan following his victory in the Genpei War — a military and institutional innovation that transferred effective political power from the imperial court at Kyoto to the warrior (samurai) class for the first time, establishing a pattern of military rule that would dominate Japanese politics for nearly seven centuries. Yoritomo deliberately located his government far from Kyoto to prevent his warriors from being seduced by court culture and losing their martial virtues.

The shogunate's administration created the institutional foundations of Japanese feudalism: shugo (military governors in each province), jito (estate managers who enforced Kamakura law on private estates), and a system of vassal relationships based on loyalty and land grants that bound the samurai class to Kamakura. The shogunate's Confucian-influenced administrative code (Joei Formulary, 1232) was the first written legal code for the warrior class, regulating property rights, inheritance, and judicial procedures in ways that shaped Japanese law for centuries. The shogunate's legitimacy faced the major challenge of the [[event:2280|Mongol invasions]] of 1274 and 1281, which it successfully repelled but at enormous financial cost that created widespread samurai discontent. The [[event:2278|Kamakura Period's]] cultural contributions — the spread of Zen Buddhism, the development of samurai aesthetics, the production of great narrative scrolls — were profound and enduring.
